MCP April 5, 2026

Connecting to an MCP server with Node.js

Learn how to connect to an MCP server using Node.js and make API requests.

My default pattern is to keep the transport layer simple and move most of the structure into readable helpers. Request builders, schema checks, and small fixture sets make it easier to express what a test is proving instead of scattering assertions across every file.

Recently I was presented with the task of testing an MCP server to ensure it was responding to requests as expected. After doing a bit of research, I installed the "MCP Inspector" tool on my local machine for experiementation. This tool provides a user-friendly interface for sending requests to the MCP server and viewing the responses, which made it easier for me to understand how the server was functioning and identify any issues that needed to be addressed. I found that using Node.js and the built-in `http` module provided a straightforward way to connect to the MCP server and make API requests. By creating a simple client that could send requests and handle responses, I was able to quickly verify the server's functionality and identify any issues that needed to be addressed.

I also like to separate validation failures into two buckets: response-shape problems and business-rule problems. That keeps test intent sharp and helps teams understand whether they’re looking at a broken contract or a broken behavior.

Once the suite is easy to trust locally, it becomes much easier to plug into CI as a reliable checkpoint instead of an occasional, flaky job that everyone reruns by instinct.
